coredata.dc:title: Triggering excitation independent fluorescence in zinc(II) incorporated carbon dots: Surface passivation of carbon dots with zinc(II) ions by microwave assisted synthesis methods
coredata.dc:creator: Gencer, Oğuz
Çeven, Ömer Faruk
Ünlü, Caner
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), versatile nanomaterials with unique photophysical and structural properties, often have excitation-dependent fluorescence due to the presence of carboxylic groups on the surface of the nanostructure. The excitation-dependent fluorescence of CDs is valuable for bioimaging, but considered undesirable for applications where monochromatic emission is required. Here in this paper, we demonstrate a green, fast and simple way to control the excitation-dependent fluorescence of CDs by passivating carboxylic groups on the surface of CDs with zinc (II) ions through a microwave synthesis method. Conventional CDs without surface passivation had excitation-dependent fluorescence, whereas zinc(II) ion-passivated CDs had excitation-independent fluorescence. Also, the surface passivation of CDs led to an increase in the quantum yield of CDs. The present work provides an inexpensive and user-friendly way to develop carbon dots with controllable emission properties by incorporating a metal ion into carbon dots through simple microwave-assisted synthesis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Surface passivation
Excitation independent emission

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of synthesis of carbon dots (CD). Ratios indicated above arrows are molar ratios. CA: Citric Acid.

a) Emission spectra of CQD, Zn(1)-CQD and Zn(10)-CQD. Each emission spectrum was collected with λexc = 350 nm. b) Emission color of CQD (Left), Zn(1)-CQD (middle) and Zn(10)-CQD (Right) under UV- Light (λexc = 366 nm).
Photoluminescence Excitation (PLE) spectra of CQD, Zn(1)-CQD and Zn(10)-CQD. Each emission spectrum was collected with λems = 440 nm. Each spectrum was normalized to 1 at highest peak point.

a) Absorbance spectrum (normalized to 1 at 310 nm) and emission spectrum (normalized to 1 at highest peak point, λexc = 350 nm) of CQDs b) Emission spectrum of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths. c) Normalized emission spectrum of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths (normalized to 1 at highest peak points).

a) Absorbance spectrum (normalized to 1 at 280 nm) and emission spectrum (normalized to 1 at highest peak point, λexc = 350 nm) of Zn(10)-CQD s b) Emission spectrum of Zn(10)-CQD s at different excitation wavelengths. c) Normalized emission spectrum of Zn(10)-CQD s at different excitation wavelengths (Normalized to 1 at highest peak points).
FTIR spectra of CQD, Zn(1)-CQD and Zn(10)-CQD.
High resolution XPS survey of carbon 1s peak (left) and oxygen 1s peak (right) of CQD, Zn(1)-CQD and Zn(10)-CQD.

head.ce:title: Triggering excitation independent fluorescence in zinc(II) incorporated carbon dots: Surface passivation of carbon dots with zinc(II) ions by microwave assisted synthesis methods

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Istanbul Technical University, Department of Nanoscience and Nanoengineering, Maslak, 34469, Istanbul, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Istanbul Technical University, Department of Nanoscience and Nanoengineering, Maslak, 34469 Istanbul, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Istanbul Technical University, Faculty of Science and Letters, Department of Chemistry, 34469 Maslak, Istanbul, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Istanbul Technical University, Faculty of Science and Letters, Department of Chemistry, 34469, Maslak, Istanbul, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Istanbul Technical University Nanotechnology Research and Application Center (ITUNano), Istanbul, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Istanbul Technical University Nanotechnology Research and Application Center (ITUNano)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Istanbul Technical University Nanotechnology Research and Application Center (ITUNano), Istanbul, Turkey

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Istanbul Technical University, Department of Nanoscience and Nanoengineering, Maslak, 34469, Istanbul, Turkey.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), versatile nanomaterials with unique photophysical and structural properties, often have excitation-dependent fluorescence due to the presence of carboxylic groups on the surface of the nanostructure. The excitation-dependent fluorescence of CDs is valuable for bioimaging, but considered undesirable for applications where monochromatic emission is required. Here in this paper, we demonstrate a green, fast and simple way to control the excitation-dependent fluorescence of CDs by passivating carboxylic groups on the surface of CDs with zinc (II) ions through a microwave synthesis method. Conventional CDs without surface passivation had excitation-dependent fluorescence, whereas zinc(II) ion-passivated CDs had excitation-independent fluorescence. Also, the surface passivation of CDs led to an increase in the quantum yield of CDs. The present work provides an inexpensive and user-friendly way to develop carbon dots with controllable emission properties by incorporating a metal ion into carbon dots through simple microwave-assisted synthesis.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are new class of luminescent carbon based nanomaterials with extremely small size (2–5 nm) and unique properties such as modifiable surface, excitation dependent emission, high quantum yield, less toxicity, etc. [1–5]. CDs are promising alternatives to metal based quantum dots and they are being used in a growing number of applications concerning biotechnology, medicine, solar cells, LED, chemosensors, cell imaging, etc. [1–8]. Therefore, the number of fundamental studies on CDs to improve their photophysical properties has increased significantly in recent years. [1–5].

CDs can be produced through top-down or bottom-up synthesis methods [1–5]. Bottom-up synthesis of carbon dots are generally performed through incomplete carbonization of a chosen carbon precursor like citric acid, glucose, etc. [9–13]. Different bottom-up synthesis techniques can be applied to synthesize CDs such as hydrothermal synthesis, microwave-assisted synthesis, etc. [1–5]. Amongst all, microwave assisted synthesis can be considered as the easiest and the most inexpensive technique to produce CDs [14–18]. Microwave-assisted synthesis of CDs can be accomplished through relatively inexpensive kitchen type microwave ovens. [18]. However, generally the CDs synthesized through microwave assisted synthesis methods have low quantum yield compared to CDs synthesized through hydrothermal synthesis methods [18]. On the other hand, the low quantum yield of CDs synthesized by microwave – assisted methods can be increased through doping CDs with non-metal atoms, such as nitrogen, boron, phosphor, sulphur, etc. [14–19]. Yet, only few studies have been done on the metal doping of CDs by microwave-assisted synthesis methods, therefore metal doping to CDs by microwave-assisted synthesis methods is still a challenge. [18].

The excitation-dependent fluorescence property of CDs is one of the most striking, well-studied and compelling of all photophysical properties. [2,4]. The excitation-dependent fluorescence feature of CDs mostly depends on the surface properties of CDs [20]. Most water-soluble CDs are synthesized using a carboxylic acid derivative compound and therefore have carboxylic groups on the surface [20]. As a consequence, the existence of carboxylic acid groups on the surface of CDs generally causes excitation dependent emission behaviour [20]. Excitation dependent emission in CDs can be helpful in biotechnology, because this feature enables CDs to be excited at longer wavelengths and therefore can be used to avoid fluorescence which can come from different organic molecules, such as amino acids [20]. On the other hand, excitation independent feature is not useful when CDs are used in devices which require single colored fluorescent materials, such as light emitting diodes and fluorescent inks [21].

Another consequence of existence of carboxylic groups on the surface of CDs is the formation of defects on the surface of CDs [20]. Defects on the surface of CDs cause the formation of defect energy states, which results in a reduction in the quantum yield of CDs. [20]. In order to decrease amount of surface defects in CDs, surface of CDs are passivated through polymers and/or small organic molecules [20]. However, passivation of the surface of a nanomaterial often requires an additional step to the experimental method and also results in a reduction in the mass yield of the product. [20]. Therefore, easy surface passivation of CDs has been a challenge and until now the potential of metals on the surface passivation of CDs has not been fully explored. [20]. At this junction, microwave assisted synthesis methods offers a rapid solution by giving opportunity to synthesize CDs within minutes with less work load, yet, successful incorporation of metals into CDs through microwave synthesis method is still a challenge [2,22].

In this study, we synthesized Zn-incorporated CDs by a simple one-step microwave-assisted synthesis method. Synthesis of Zn-incorporated CDs was performed within minutes unlike hydrothermal synthesis methods at ambient conditions and single step. Zn2+ ions passivated the surface of CDs and caused an increase in the quantum yield of CDs. Moreover, the excitation-dependent emission properties of CDs were lost upon Zn passivation. This study is the first to suggest that microwave-assisted synthesis can be used to passivate CDs via a metal ion, in this case Zn2+ ion.

In this study, the microwave-assisted synthesis method was used to synthesize carbon dots by using citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O) and zinc chloride (ZnCl2) as carbon (C) and zinc (Zn) sources, respectively. Carbon dots (CQD), zinc incorporated carbon dots with 1:1 M ratio of citric acid: ZnCl2 (Zn(1)-CQD) and zinc incorporated carbon dots with 10:1 M ratio of citric acid: ZnCl2 (Zn(10)-CQD) were synthesized as follows.

For the synthesis of CQDs, 100 mg of citric acid monohydrate was added into a 25 ml flat bottom flask, and then the flask was placed into a domestic microwave oven (SAMSUNG/Model No: MS23F300EEK) and heated at 800 W for 20 min. Then, while the product has low viscosity, the product was dropped into a petri dish and was left for cooling. Afterwards, the product in gel form with a yellowish colour was obtained. Finally, the CQD product was dissolved in 10 ml ultrapure water and transferred into a 50 ml beaker, purified by filtering through 0.22 μm pored filters and centrifuging at 14000 RPM for 30 min, dried and kept at +2 °C for further analysis.

For the synthesis of Zn(1)-CQD, 0.5 mmol of citric acid monohydrate and 0.5 mmol of ZnCl2 was added into a 25 ml flat bottom flask, and then the flask was placed into a domestic microwave oven (SAMSUNG/Model No: MS23F300EEK) and heated at 800 W for 20 min. Then, while the product has low viscosity, the product was dropped into a petri dish and was left for cooling. Afterwards, the product in gel form with a transparent white colour was obtained. Finally, Zn(1)-CQD product was dissolved in 10 ml ultrapure water and transferred into a 50 ml beaker, purified by filtering through 0.22 μm pored filters and centrifuging at 14000 RPM for 30 min, dried and kept at +2 °C for further analysis.

For the synthesis of Zn(10)-CQD, 0.5 mmol of citric acid monohydrate and 5 mmol of ZnCl2 was added into a 25 ml flat bottom flask, and then the flask was placed into a domestic microwave oven (SAMSUNG/Model No: MS23F300EEK) and heated at 800 W for 20 min. Then, while the product has low viscosity, the product was dropped into a petri dish and was left for cooling. Afterwards, the product in gel form with an opaque white colour was obtained. Finally, Zn(10)-CQD product was dissolved in 10 ml ultrapure water and transferred into a 50 ml beaker, purified by filtering through 0.22 μm pored filters and centrifuging at 14000 RPM for 30 min, dried and kept at +2 °C for further analysis.

To characterize structural and optical properties of carbon dots, UV–VIS spectrophotometer, Fluorescence Spectrometer, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) were used.
The absorption spectra of CQD, Zn(1)-CQD and Zn(10)-CQD were recorded by the Scinco Neosys-2000 Single-Beam Ultraviolet-Visible Spectrophotometer (UV-VIS).

The Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrofluorometer was used to record the emission spectra of quantum dots. The emission spectrum for each sample was recorded by using excitation wavelengths varied between 350 and 450 nm. In order to prevent the errors resulted from self-absorption, each sample was dissolved in 10 ml of ultrapure water after purification then diluted until optical density was under 0.1. Each sample was observed under 366 nm UV-Light in order to determine the emission colour. Photoluminescence excitation spectrum of each CD was collected at emission wavelength (λems) of 440 nm, which is a common wavelength that exists in peaks of each emission spectrum.

Quantum yields of CQD, Zn(1)-CQD and Zn(10)-CQD were measured by using Coumarin 102 in ethanol as a standard (the reference quantum yield was 0.93) [23]. All of the optical characterizations were performed on aqueous solutions of carbon dots.
For the structural analysis, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) and X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) were used.

The chemical compositions of CQD, Zn(1)-CQD and Zn(10)-CQD were determined by XPS. Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-Ray Photoelectron Spectrometer with a monochromatic 1486.68 eV Al Kα X-Ray line-source and a 400 μm beam size was used. In order to obtain detailed information about the bonding characterization of CQD, Zn(1)-CQD and Zn(10)-CQD, each sample was characterized by Attenuated Total Reflectance FTIR (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy. The FTIR spectra of the purified carbon dots were measured in the range of 800–4000 cm−1 by using a Perkin Elmer ATR-FTIR Spectrophotometer. The shape and the size of the carbon dots were determined by TEM analysis. TEM images were taken by JEOL JEM-1220 at an acceleration voltage of 100 kV.

Synthesis of carbon dots through microwave assisted methods is a widely used technique due to easy and cheap synthesis conditions [18]. On the other hand, the relatively lower quantum yields (about 20%) of microwave-synthesized carbon dots compared to the quantum yields of Cd based quantum dots (above 50%) led to the search for ways to increase the quantum yield of carbon dots with different methods such as heteroatom doping and surface passivation. [11,18]. Especially, doping carbon dots with non-metal atoms, such as nitrogen, boron, sulphur, phosphorus, resulted in significant increase in quantum yields of carbon dots [18,20,24–27]. Generally, hydrothermal, solvothermal and microwave based bottom – up synthesis methods are used to dope carbon dots with heteroatoms or passivate surface of carbon dots [20,24–27]. However, the doping or passivation of carbon dots with metal ions by microwave-assisted synthesis has not been a widely studied topic [18,20].

In our study, a modified microwave assisted synthesis of metal incorporated carbon dots was performed by using citric acid monohydrate as carbon precursor and ZnCl2 as Zinc precursor (Fig. 1 ). Conventional synthesis conditions, which were used to synthesize carbon dots and non-metallic heteroatom doped carbon dots, were used to synthesize metal incorporated carbon dots [18]. Metal incorporated carbon dots were synthesized by microwave treatment of aqueous solution of ZnCl2 and citric acid (Fig. 1). Two different molar ratios of citric acid: ZnCl2 were chosen to observe prominent changes in emission and structure properties in zinc-doped CDs; 1:1 and 1:10.

As the emission spectrum of conventional carbon dots (CQD) and emission spectrum of Zinc incorporated carbon dots with Citric acid:ZnCl2 molar ratio 1:1 (Zn(1)-CQD) were compared, it was observed that the emission of Zn(1)-CQDs was almost quenched (Fig. 2 ). However, when the molar ratio of Citric acid:ZnCl2 was changed to 1:10 (Zn(10)-CQD), the emission peak reappeared again with a higher intensity (Fig. 2). The quantum yield of Zn(10)-CQD increased significantly compared to that of CQD; the calculated quantum yield of CQDs was 20% and the calculated quantum yield of Zn(10)-CQD was 51%. The emission peak of CQD was observed at 440 nm with full width at half maximum (FWHM) 110 nm. However, the emission peak of Zn(10)-CQD was narrower (FWHM = 88 nm) and observed at lower energy region, the peak was at 455 nm (Fig. 2). Also, the Zn(10)-CQDs did not lose their brightness for at least 2 months. Only a slight decrease in emission spectrum of Zn(10)-CQDs were observed two months after the synthesis of carbon dots (Fig. S1). All these observations suggested that incorporation of Zn into CDs resulted in increase of non-radiative transitions most likely due to causing heterogeneity in the structure of CDs and resulted in decrease in quantum yield of CDs. However, as the amount of Zn increased, the structure of CDs became homogeneous and as a result the quantum yield of the Zn-incorporated CDs increased even more than it used to be compared to convetional CDs. All these claims were checked by further optical - structural analyses and strong evidences were found and discussed further.

The photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectrum of CQD at λems = 440 nm possessed 2 peaks at 288 nm and 343 nm. As the Zn was added to system, the PLE spectrum changed dramatically (Fig. 3 ). PLE spectrum of Zn(1)-CQD at λems = 440 nm had 3 peaks at 264 nm, 325 nm and 400 nm (Fig. 3). As the Zn ratio was increased, the PLE spectrum changed even more. PLE spectrum of Zn(10)-CQD at λems = 440 nm had 2 peaks at 281 nm and 372 nm (Fig. 3). The changes in the PL and PLE spectra of the carbon dots with the addition of Zn2+ implied that the addition of Zn2+ initially caused formation of defects in the structure of carbon dots by giving rise to formation of heterogeneous zones, therefore resulted in disturbance in the energy states of carbon dots, and caused a significant decrease in the quantum efficiency of Zn(1)-CQD and significant changes in the PLE spectrum of Zn(1)-CQD. However, as the amount of Zn2+ increased further, the structure of the carbon dots became homogeneous again, therefore the quantum yield of Zn incorporated CDs increased significantly, and the PLE spectra of CQD and Zn(10)-CQD became similar in shape although significant shifts were observed in their peak positions, which has been attributed to structural differences between CQDs and Zn(10)-CQDs. All these observations showed that excessive Zn2+ addition caused either Zn doping in the carbon dot structure or surface passivation of carbon dots.

As further analysis was performed on the photophysical properties of CQDs and Zn(10)-CQDs, even more dramatic differences were observed. CQDs have a typical absorbance spectrum usually observed for carbon dots [1–5]; a band-edge absorption band which appeared as a shoulder around 330 nm and a very intense, continuous absorption characteristic above 300 nm (Fig. 4 ). CQDs had excitation dependent emission, which could appear due to oxidation of surface of CQDs [20]. When CQDs were excited with λexc = 350 nm, the emission peak appeared at 440 nm. As the excitation peak switched to higher wavelengths, 400 nm and 450 nm, the emission maximum shifted to 475 nm and 500 nm, respectively (Fig. 4). Also, the emission intensity of CQDs decreased gradually as the excitation wavelength was shifted to higher wavelengths (Fig. 4). All of these observations were expected results and typical CQD emission characteristics, which were reported in literature [20].

On the other hand, the emission characteristics of Zn(10)-CQDs were mostly different from those of CQDs. The absorption spectrum of Zn(10)-CQDs was similar to the absorption spectrum of CQDs (Figs. 4 and 5 ). On the other hand, the emission spectra of Zn(10)-CQDs showed a nearly excitation-independent property. The emission peaks of Zn(10)-CQDs were at 455 nm, 457 nm and 459 nm when λexc were 350 nm, 375 nm and 400 nm (Fig. 5). It should be noted that the emission of Zn(10)-CQDs almost completely disappeared at λexc = 450 nm (Fig. 5) and the emission intensity of Zn(10)-CQDs was at highest when λexc = 375 nm (Fig. 5). The most prominent observation for Zn(10)-CQDs, the excitation independent emission behaviour, pointed out that as the amount of Zn2+ increased, the surface of the carbon dots became passivated with Zn2+. Thus, the excitation dependent emission behaviour of CQDs lost and instead carbon dots with excitation independent emission characteristics formed.

In order to determine size of carbon dots and clarify the structural changes in Zn(10)-CQDs, TEM, FTIR and XPS measurements were conducted. TEM results of Zn(10)-CQDs showed that Zn(10)-CQDs had size in the range of 2–4 nm with uniform size distribution (Figs. 6 , S2). This size distribution is in agreement with size of traditional carbon dots which are synthesized through either microwave assisted synthesis methods or hydrothermal synthesis methods before [16,19]. As size of Zn(10) - CQDs compared with size of CQDs, whose TEM characterization was performed before in a different work [16], it was observed that incorporation of Zn to CQD did not affect the size of carbon dots (Fig. 6.)

The FTIR spectrum of CQDs, Zn(10)-CQDs and Zn(1)-CQDs showed dramatic differences (Fig. 7 ). FTIR spectrum of CQDs possessed 2 dominant peaks at 1185 cm−1 and 1701 cm−1, which were attributed to CO stretching and CO stretching, respectively. These two peaks started to disappear in FTIR spectrum of Zn(1)-CQD and completely disappeared in FTIR spectrum of Zn(10)-CQDs. Instead, a new peak emerged at 1609 cm−1 in FTIR spectrum of Zn(1)-CQDs and FTIR spectrum of Zn(10)-CQDs, which was attributed to asymmetric stretching of COO− groups observed in zinc carboxylate salts [28]. The ratio of intensities of peaks at 1609 cm−1 and 1701 cm−1 increased in the favour of 1609 cm−1 as the Zn amount was increased, and the peak at 1701 cm−1 completely disappeared in Zn(10)-CQDs (Fig. S3). These observations implied that carboxylic groups in CQDs started to be passivated as Zn2+ ions were added to citric acid, and in Zn(10)-CQDs the carboxylic groups were completely passivated. It should be noted that all carbon dots had typical –OH band at 3100–3400 cm−1 and CH2 wagging peak at 1402 cm−1. Also, the Zn – O vibration peak around 460 cm−1 was observed in FTIR spectrum of each Zn incorporated CQDs (Fig. S3).

Complete XPS survey of carbon dots showed that carbon, oxygen and zinc atoms existed in Zn incorporated CDs, but only carbon and oxygen existed in non Zn incorporated CDs (Fig. S4). HR-XPS spectrum of carbon 1s peak of carbon dots revealed even more striking results (Fig. 8 ). To understand the changes in bonding characterization carbon in carbon dots, HR-XPS spectrum of carbon atom in CQDs, Zn(1)-CQDs and Zn(10)-CQDs were deeply analysed (Fig. 8). Carbon HR-XPS spectrum of CQDs possessed 3 main peaks; two dominant peaks at 285. 9 eV and 289.5 eV, one peak at 287.1 eV with less intensity compared to intensities of peaks at 285.9 eV and 289.5 eV. The dominant peaks at 285.9 and 289.5 are attributed to aliphatic/graphitic carbons and COO−, respectively [29–31]. The peak at 287.1 eV was attributed to CO bonding [29–31] (Fig. 8). As Zn was incorporated into carbon dot structure, the peak positions and peak intensities in HR-XPS spectrum of carbon changed (Fig. 8). The peak at 289.5 eV in 1s C HR-XPS spectrum of CQDs slightly shifted to 289.1 eV in HR-XPS spectra of Zn(1)-CQDs and Zn(10)-CQDs. More importantly, the intensity of the peak at 289.1 eV, which was attributed to COO-, decreased gradually as Zn amount increased (Fig. 8). This observation indicated that as Zn was incorporated into CQD structure, Zn ions mostly passivate COO- groups. The peak at 285.9 eV, which was attributed to aliphatic/graphitic carbons, in HR-XPS spectrum of CQDs shifted to 285.1 eV and 284.7 eV in HR-XPS spectrum of Zn(1)-CQDs and Zn(10)-CQDs, respectively. This observation implied that Zn affected the bonding characterization of carbon and metal-carbon interactions started to be observed in XPS spectrum, since metal – carbon peaks in 1s C HR-XPS spectrum were observed around 283–284 eV [32]. It should be noted that the peak at 287.1 eV, which was attributed to CO, was observed in HR-XPS spectrum of all carbon dots (Fig. 8). To sum up, the COO− groups in CQDs became passivated as Zn was incorporated in CQD structure and thus, surface of the carbon dots became much more stable which affected quantum yield and excitation dependent emission characteristic of CQDs.

Also, Oxygen HR-XPS spectrum of each CD showed significant differences (Fig. 8). O 1s peak has shifted to lower binding energy as Zn was added to the system (Fig. 8). This observation indicated that Zn interacted with oxygen in CD, because Zn-O interaction is observed at lower eV compared to carbon – oxygen interactions [33]. Also, full width at half maximum (FWHM) of O 1s peaks slightly decreased to 2 eV from 2.8 eV as zinc was added to structure (for CQDs; 2.8 eV, Zn(1)-CQDs; 2.4 eV and Zn(10)-CQDs; 2 eV). This observation was attributed to decrease in CO bond character and increase in zinc – oxygen interaction. In 1s O HR-XPS spectrum, CO double bond is observed at 533 eV, CO bond is observed around 531–532 eV and zinc oxygen interaction is observed around 513.5 nm [33]. As was observed in HR-XPS spectrum of C 1s, the CO bond peak slightly disappeared in O 1s spectrum and zinc – oxygen interaction started to become more dominant and observable.

Carboxylic groups on the surface of the carbon dots were passivated with Zn+2 ions by microwave synthesis methods. After passivation, the excitation dependent emission characteristics of the carbon dots disappeared. The addition of 1 equivalent of ZnCl2 relative to citric acid caused quenching in the emission of carbon dots, but the quantum yield of carbon dots increased to 51% as 10 equivalents of ZnCl2 were used. The fact that all experiments were carried out in a kitchen type microwave oven within 20 min shows that surface passivation of carbon dots can be done easily and quickly with less effort.
Oğuz Gençer: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - Original Draft, Visualization, Formal analysis.
Ömer Faruk Çeven: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – Revision Draft, Visualization, Formal analysis.
Caner Ünlü: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing - Original Draft, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervising, Visualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

We wish to draw the attention of the Editor to the following facts which may be considered as potential conflicts of interest and to significant financial contributions to this work. We wish to confirm that there are no known conflicts of interest associated with this publication and there has been no significant financial support for this work that could have influenced its outcome. We confirm that we have given due consideration to the protection of intellectual property associated with this work and that there are no impediments to publication, including the timing of publication, with respect to intellectual property. In so doing we confirm that we have followed the regulations of our institutions concerning intellectual property. We understand that the Corresponding Author is the sole contact for the Editorial process (including Editorial Manager and direct communications with the office). He/she is responsible for communicating with the other authors about progress, submissions of revisions and final approval of proofs. We confirm that we have provided a current, correct email address which is accessible by the Corresponding Author and which has been configured to accept email from canerunlu@itu.edu.tr. Signed by corresponding author on behalf of all authors:
This work was supported by Istanbul Technical University – Scientific Research Projects Unit (ITU-BAP) [TGA-2019-41938].

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: C.Ü has received research grants from ITU-BAP (Istanbul Technical University – Scientific Research Projects Unit). The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Dr. Ahmet Gül for support and letting use of UV-VIS and FTIR spectrometers. We also thank Dr. Bünyamin Karagöz and his laboratory crew for support and letting use of Fluorescence Spectrometer. We would like to thank Prof. Dr. İsmail Koyuncu for the FTIR measurements and data analysis. The TEM measurements and data analysis were performed in Eskişehir Osmangazi University – Central Research Laboratory and Application Center.
